section: Microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) Be low-density steels

context_update.summary: This chunk contains the title, abstract and introduction. It introduces Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x=0,0.5,1.0,1.5) low-density steels in as-cast and hot-rolled (1050 °C) conditions. The abstract reports that increasing Be transforms the microstructure from single austenite to dual-phase austenite+ferrite. Be forms needle-like Be–Fe precipitates in as-cast state and spherical/dot-like after hot rolling. At 0.5 wt% Be the hot-rolled structure improves strength and ductility via solid-solution strengthening and grain refinement, while elongation slightly decreases with Be content. Density drops to ~6.25 g/cm³ at 1.5 wt% Be, 19.87% lower than a standard 7.82 g/cm³ steel and 3.8% lower than the base Fe–Mn–Al–C (6.50 g/cm³). Strength, hardness and elongation stay at a high level for 0.5–1.5 Be steels, unprecedented in previous Fe–Mn–Al–C systems. The introduction motivates the use of beryllium (density 1.848 g/cm³) because Al and C alone cannot reduce density below 6.40 g/cm³. The study systematically investigates the effect of 0–1.5 wt% Be and the precipitate evolution in as-cast and hot-rolled states. Subsequent chunks will present experimental details, microstructural characterization and mechanical property data.
properties: elongation slightly decreases with Be content
strength, hardness, elongation remain at high level for 0.5–1.5Be
mechanisms: solid solution strengthening and grain refinement at 0.5Be hot-rolled

context_update.summary: This chunk contains the latter part of the introduction, the complete experimental section, and the beginning of the results (as-cast microstructure and hardness). Samples Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x=0–1.5 wt%) labeled 0Be to 1.5Be were vacuum induction melted and hot-rolled at 1050 °C in 17 passes (83% reduction) with water quenching. Testing methods include tensile (ASTM E8, 5×10⁻⁴ m·s⁻¹), Vickers hardness (500 gf, 25-point average), Archimedes density, EBSD, OM, SEM, TEM, XRD (Cu Kα, 2°/min, 20–100°) and DSC (10 °C/min). Initial as-cast results show: (i) XRD (Fig. 1) identifies austenite + precipitates in 0Be/0.5Be, and austenite+ferrite+precipitates in 1.0Be/1.5Be, with ferrite (110) peak growing at 44.67° as Be increases; Be–Fe compounds Be11Fe, Be12Fe (Fe:Be 18.7:1 in 0.5Be) and additionally Be2Fe in high-Be alloys; (ii) OM (Fig. 2) reveals Chinese character-shaped Be–Fe phase above 0.5 wt% Be and ferrite; (iii) TEM confirms micrometre-sized Be12Fe (5–10 µm) and AlFe₃ diffraction in 1.5Be; (iv) matrix hardness increases with Be (Table 2, Fig. 5). The Be–Fe phase hardness in 0.5Be could not be measured. Subsequent chunks should address hot-rolled microstructures, tensile properties, density data and processing–structure–property correlations.
key_variables: as-cast condition (hot-rolled state not yet characterized in this chunk)
microstructure: as-cast 0.5Be: austenite + Be11Fe, Be12Fe precipitates; no ferrite
as-cast 1.0Be and 1.5Be: austenite + ferrite + Be2Fe, Be12Fe; Chinese character-shaped Be–Fe phase
properties: as-cast matrix hardness increases with Be content
hardness of austenite and ferrite (where present) increases with Be
Be–Fe phase hardness in 0.5Be not measurable
mechanisms: Be promotes ferrite formation
Be forms Fe–Be intermetallic compounds (Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe)
AlFe3 observed in 1.5Be matrix

section: 3.1. Microstructure and mechanical properties of as-cast states
previous_context_summary:
Summary: Subsequent chunks will present experimental details, microstructural characterization and mechanical property data. This chunk contains the latter part of the introduction, the complete experimental section, and the beginning of the results (as-cast microstructure and hardness). Samples Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x=0–1.5 wt%) labeled 0Be to 1.5Be were vacuum induction melted and hot-rolled at 1050 °C in 17 passes (83% reduction) with water quenching. Testing methods include tensile (ASTM E8, 5×10⁻⁴ m·s⁻¹), Vickers hardness (500 gf, 25-point average), Archimedes density, EBSD, OM, SEM, TEM, XRD (Cu Kα, 2°/min, 20–100°) and DSC (10 °C/min). Initial as-cast results show: (i) XRD (Fig. 1) identifies austenite + precipitates in 0Be/0.5Be, and austenite+ferrite+precipitates in 1.0Be/1.5Be, with ferrite (110) peak growing at 44.67° as Be increases; Be–Fe compounds Be11Fe, Be12Fe (Fe:Be 18.7:1 in 0.5Be) and additionally Be2Fe in high-Be alloys; (ii) OM (Fig. 2) reveals Chinese character-shaped Be–Fe phase above 0.5 wt% Be and ferrite; (iii) TEM confirms micrometre-sized Be12Fe (5–10 µm) and AlFe₃ diffraction in 1.5Be; (iv) matrix hardness increases with Be (Table 2, Fig. 5). The Be–Fe phase hardness in 0.5Be could not be measured. Subsequent chunks should address hot-rolled microstructures, tensile properties, density data and processing–structure–property correlations.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers as-cast microhardness (Table 2, Fig. 5) and extended characterization of hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe steels. As-cast austenite hardness rises with Be (0Be 453.31 to 1.5Be 479.74 HV); ferrite and Be–Fe hardness are provided where measurable. The rolled condition is examined extensively: DSC shows a large drop in phase transformation temperature upon Be addition (~120 °C from 1278.8 °C in 0Be, with minor further decreases). XRD of rolled strips identifies phase progression: 0Be (austenite+carbide), 0.5Be (Be11Fe), 1.0Be (Be11Fe+Be12Fe), 1.5Be (Be11Fe+Be12Fe+Be2Fe+Be2C). OM reveals persistent needle-like Be–Fe compounds (altered size/distribution) and annealing twins. SEM/EDS on 1.5Be resolves elongated ferrite with Mn,Al partitioning. Tensile properties show 0.5Be achieves 40.47% elongation, with ductility declining above 0.5% Be; yield strength follows increase–decrease–increase trend, opposite to tensile strength. Work hardening rate initially decreases then increases with Be. Rolled hardness (Table 4) gives specific values for austenite and ferrite. Fractography indicates increasing cleavage with Be. EBSD (Fig. 13) demonstrates ferrite fraction rises with Be and stress concentrates at precipitate interfaces. Grain sizes: 0Be ~12–18 μm, 0.5Be mostly >20 μm, 1.0Be ~11 μm, 1.5Be ~13 μm. TEM (Fig. 15) proves austenitic matrix in all rolled alloys, with κ-carbides in 0Be and Be2C (size increasing with Be) in Be-containing grades. Further sections may rely on specific tensile data from Table 3 and processing–structure–property correlations.
microstructure: needle-like Be–Fe compounds persisting after rolling
κ-carbides in 0Be, Be2C in Be-containing steels
mechanisms: Be significantly lowers phase transformation temperature
Be promotes ferrite formation and refines/alters precipitate morphology
small amount of Be–Fe phase enhances plasticity
stress concentration at precipitate/matrix interfaces

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the Discussion section on mechanical properties (as-cast and rolled) and initial microstructure description for rolled strips. Hardness trends in as-cast state are recalled; rolled tensile properties from Table 3 and microhardness from Table 4 are presented. Strengthening mechanisms are discussed: solid-solution strengthening in 0.5Be, ferrite-induced plasticity reduction in higher Be alloys, precipitation strengthening from carbides and Be–Fe compounds, and dislocation strengthening evidenced by TEM and EBSD KAM. Fracture behavior shifts from ductile to mixed ductile-cleavage with increasing Be; spherical precipitates ~2 µm nucleate microvoids. In dual-phase steels, cracks initiate in brittle ferrite and are arrested by austenite. OM (Fig. 8) confirms annealing twins and ferrite in 1.0Be and 1.5Be. The discussion establishes processing–property relationships for rolled conditions, linking Be content to strength, hardness, ductility, and fracture. Future sections may extend these correlations to microstructural evolution mechanisms.
microstructure: rolled 1.0Be and 1.5Be: dual-phase austenite + ferrite + Be–Fe phase + Be2C
spherical precipitates ~2 µm at dimple bottoms in high-Be steel
dislocation density increases with Be content
properties: fracture: ductile for low Be; mixed plastic-brittle for 1.0Be and 1.5Be

ferrite formation reduces plasticity
carbide and Be–Fe precipitation strengthens alloy
Be2C presence in all Be-containing rolled steels

This chunk covers the Discussion section on mechanical properties (as-cast and rolled) and initial microstructure description for rolled strips. Hardness trends in as-cast state are recalled; rolled tensile properties from Table 3 and microhardness from Table 4 are presented. Strengthening mechanisms are discussed: solid-solution strengthening in 0.5Be, ferrite-induced plasticity reduction in higher Be alloys, precipitation strengthening from carbides and Be–Fe compounds, and dislocation strengthening evidenced by TEM and EBSD KAM. Fracture behavior shifts from ductile to mixed ductile-cleavage with increasing Be; spherical precipitates ~2 µm nucleate microvoids. In dual-phase steels, cracks initiate in brittle ferrite and are arrested by austenite. OM (Fig. 8) confirms annealing twins and ferrite in 1.0Be and 1.5Be. The discussion establishes processing–property relationships for rolled conditions, linking Be content to strength, hardness, ductility, and fracture. Future sections may extend these correlations to microstructural evolution mechanisms.
Material systems: Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe; austenite; ferrite; Be–Fe intermetallic; austenite (γ); ferrite (δ); Be12Fe; Be11Fe
Sample aliases: 0Be; 0.5Be; 1.0Be; 1.5Be
Processing route: as-cast; hot rolling at 1050 °C; vacuum induction melting; hot rolling at 1050 °C, 17 passes, 83% reduction; water quenching
Key variables: Be content: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%; processing state: as-cast vs. hot-rolled; Be content (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 wt%); as-cast condition (hot-rolled state not yet characterized in this chunk); rolled condition
Microstructure: single austenite in 0Be; dual-phase austenite+ferrite at higher Be; needle-like Be–Fe in as-cast; spherical/dot-like Be–Fe after hot rolling; as-cast 0Be: fully austenitic; as-cast 0.5Be: austenite + Be11Fe, Be12Fe precipitates; no ferrite; as-cast 1.0Be and 1.5Be: austenite + ferrite + Be2Fe, Be12Fe; Chinese character-shaped Be–Fe phase; ferrite content increases with Be
Properties: density ~6.25 g/cm³ at 1.5Be; 19.87% density reduction vs. 7.82 g/cm³ steel; 3.8% reduction vs. base Fe–Mn–Al–C (6.50 g/cm³); 0.5Be hot-rolled improves strength and ductility; elongation slightly decreases with Be content; strength, hardness, elongation remain at high level for 0.5–1.5Be; as-cast matrix hardness increases with Be content; hardness of austenite and ferrite (where present) increases with Be
Mechanisms: solid solution strengthening and grain refinement at 0.5Be hot-rolled; Be promotes ferrite formation; Be forms Fe–Be intermetallic compounds (Be11Fe, Be12Fe, Be2Fe); AlFe3 observed in 1.5Be matrix; Be significantly lowers phase transformation temperature; Be promotes ferrite formation and refines/alters precipitate morphology; small amount of Be–Fe phase enhances plasticity; stress concentration at precipitate/matrix interfaces

context_update.summary: This chunk covers microstructural characterization of as-cast and hot-rolled Fe–30Mn–10Al–1.5C–xBe (x=0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5) steels. Rolled-strip EBSD maps (IQ, IPF, KAM, Phase) are introduced in Fig. 13, and grain-size statistics in Fig. 14. In the as-cast state, Be precipitates as ancient-Chinese-character morphology at γ/δ boundaries; spherical, rod-like, and needle-like compounds degrade properties. Be12Fe is confirmed by SADP in 0.5Be (Fig. 3), and large intersecting precipitates appear in 1.5Be (Fig. 4). The Fe–Be phase diagram shows negligible solid solubility of Be in austenite or ferrite at room temperature. At higher Be contents (1.0Be, 1.5Be), increased Be–Fe phase reduces both austenite and ferrite fractions, and ferrite grains are finer in 1.5Be. After hot rolling, Be compounds become more numerous and dispersed; needle-like particles decrease while punctate ones increase. In fully austenitic 0Be and 0.5Be, grain size grows with Be; in dual-phase 1.0Be and 1.5Be, ferrite refines overall grain size. Ferrite nucleates at austenite-grain centers from the melt, pointing to near-rapid solidification. Al and Be shrink the austenite region. Table 5 compiles atomic sizes and solid-solution types. These microstructural foundations will link to subsequent property discussions.
key_variables: cooling rate (slow as-cast vs. water quench)
microstructure: as-cast: ancient Chinese character Be precipitates, spherical/rod/needle compounds
hot-rolled: increased dispersed Be compounds, needle-to-punctate transition
0Be and 0.5Be fully austenitic; 1.0Be and 1.5Be dual-phase with ferrite at grain centers
grain size increases with Be in austenitic steels, decreases with ferrite in dual-phase steels
mechanisms: Be and Al expand ferrite phase region, shrink austenite
near-rapid solidification: ferrite nucleates at austenite grain centers
hot deformation transforms compound morphology and disperses them
excess Be precipitates as spot-like compounds on ferrite
lattice distortion from solute atoms cited but not detailed

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the rolled strip characterization, lattice distortion analysis, and conclusions. The atomic radii and solid solution types of elements in the steel are given (Table 5): Fe 124 pm, Mn 124 pm, Al 143 pm, Be 111 pm, C 77 pm; Mn, Al, Be form substitutional solid solutions, C interstitial. The chunk derives the infinite solid solution criterion (Δr<8% and identical crystal structure), which Be does not meet. Key lattice distortion mechanisms: Be causes lattice contraction, Al causes expansion (Fig. 18). Lattice distortion energy leads to instability and forms Be–Fe phases when solubility is exceeded. Be substitution displaces C atoms to precipitate as carbides. Density reduction differs: Al reduces density via outward expansion, Be via inward contraction is ineffective despite Be's low density (1.87 g/cm³). The Fe–Be phase diagram shows Be solubility in austenite <1%, with compounds precipitating; ferrite dissolves more Be (Fig. 19). At 0.5Be, XRD shows no ferrite; at 1.0Be ferrite appears. After hot rolling, the as-cast Chinese-character Be–Fe phase becomes spherical/point-shaped (Conclusion 4). Measured densities are 6.47, 6.36, 6.29 g/cm³ for 0.5Be, 1.0Be, 1.5Be. Mechanical trends: small Be suppresses carbides and improves strength/plasticity; increasing ferrite with Be decreases yield strength/hardness in general but in dual-phase 1.0Be and 1.5Be, strength rises while elongation drops. The strength-ductility-density comparative diagram (Fig. 20) lists products: 45.5, 32.8, 22.0 GPa% at densities 6.4, 6.4, 6.3 g/cm³. TEM (Figs. 15, 16) and schematic (Fig. 17) document microstructural features.
microstructure: spherical and point-shaped Be–Fe after hot rolling
carbide precipitation at surface from C expulsion
properties: yield strength and hardness decrease with ferrite increase
yield and tensile strength increase in dual-phase 1.0Be/1.5Be, elongation decreases
density reduction not significant from Be
mechanisms: Be lattice contraction vs Al lattice expansion
size factor Δr for solid solution
Be solubility limit in austenite <1%
C atom expulsion from interstitial sites due to Be substitution
lattice distortion energy leads to phase instability and compound formation
Be–Fe phase nucleation in austenite or ferrite formation
